Motion For Leave To Conduct Financial Discovery On Defendants A Nhi Le Qu Yan Kuang A-Nhi Le Trustee Of The Qu Yan Kuang And A Nhi Le Revocable Trust And Qu Yan Kuang Trustee Of The Qu Yan Kuang And A Nhi Le Revocable Trust
Off Calendar Aug-26-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: PLAINTIFF ENKHDUL BATSUKH's, AN INDIVIDUAL, Motion For Leave To Conduct Financial Discovery On Defendants A Nhi Le Qu Yan Kuang A-Nhi Le Trustee Of The Qu Yan Kuang And A Nhi Le Revocable Trust And Qu Yan Kuang Trustee Of The Qu Yan Kuang And A Nhi Le Revocable Trust.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. On August 2,2024, plaintiff filed a motion for leave of court to conduct financial discovery from defendants pursuant to Civil Code section 3295, subsection (c). Plaintiff contends that she can and has "established that there is a substantial probability that the plaintiff will prevail on the claim pursuant to Section 3294." In support of her motion, plaintiff offers her complaint, several DBI complaint forms, excerpts from the defendant's deposition and expert witness opinions about the condition of the premises rented by defendants to plaintiff for a period of years. Essentially, plaintiff contends the addition of an illegal unit in the garage (plaintiff rented the premises above the garage) together with building and health code violations by defendants over an extended period satisfies her burden under the cited provision and entitles her to entry of an order granting leave to conduct financial discovery at this point in time. Complicating plaintiff's motion for pretrial financial discovery is the fact that trial appears imminent. On August 20, 2024, a pretrial order was issued by Judge Karnow setting forth the essential ground rules for the trial. The parties have been exchanging salvos of motions in limine, and trial could commence at any time. Under these circumstances, putting aside the parties' dispute over when discovery closed, the financial discovery plaintiff seeks and the jury trial of her complaint are on a collision course. Moreover, the Court is not convinced the evidence plaintiff offers in support of her motion satisfies the criteria quoted above. It is not easy to satisfy the oppression, fraud or malice standard of Section 3294. Plaintiff may have been treated unfairly by defendants, and various tenancy-related provisions of law, rule and regulation violated, some egregiously. The Court, however, is unsure whether that evidence will push the rock up to the peak of the Section 3294 hill. It is likely that the issue of punitive damages will be bifurcated (where is the defendants' motion in limine on that point?) from the issues of liability and compensatory damages. In phase one of the trial, the jury would be asked to decide whether one or more of the criteria of Section 3294 have been satisfied. Only if the jury so finds would the trial proceed to phase two, deciding the amount of punitive damages. Often there is a pause of several days between the two phases, during which financial discovery could be conducted including amassing evidence of the value of the property leased to plaintiff, something that could have been done well before trial. Thus, seeking leave to conduct pretrial financial discovery is only one way to obtain evidence as to the defendants' financial worth for purposes of assessing a fair award of punitive damages. For all of these reasons, plaintiff's motion for leave to conduct pretrial financial discovery is DENIED, without prejudice to being renewed when and if the jury has returned a verdict including a finding of oppression, fraud or malice.

Counsel for defendant A-Nhi Le is directed to prepare a proposed order consistent with the foregoing and submit it to psw@hassard.com for review and signature.

Judge Pro Tem: Philip Ward. Clerk: R. Woods. Not Reported. (525/JPT)
